museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: April is a careful Breakout drawer: some modern summaries point here, but the exhibit keeps the release month cautious.
museum.curatorNote: Breakout is the anchor, but the month is marked with source caution rather than overconfidence.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments from a very early videogame year, with broad context separated from confident month-level claims.

items.title: Breakout becomes the year's design landmark
items.body: Atari's Breakout turns the Pong grammar into a single-player brick-breaking ritual.
items.sourceNote: Some sources use April 1976; The Strong and arcade histories anchor the game to 1976.

items.title: Jobs and Wozniak enter the arcade myth
items.body: Breakout's development story links Atari, Steve Jobs and Steve Wozniak, making it part of both arcade and personal-computer lore.
items.sourceNote: The Strong and Breakout histories discuss Jobs/Wozniak involvement.

items.title: The single-player arcade skill loop sharpens
items.body: Breakout is not just Pong without a second player: it makes progression, concentration and failure feel cleanly personal.

items.title: UK players meet such games as public machines
items.body: The lived British route is still the cabinet, not the software shop.

items.title: The home cartridge future remains just ahead
items.body: Channel F has not yet changed the home category from fixed appliances to programmable cartridges.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: A deliberately sparse shelf: only releases supported by the sources reviewed are displayed.

items.title: Breakout
items.body: Atari's brick-breaking classic. Included here as the month's key discussion object, but exact public availability is kept cautious.
items.certainty: verified year; April appears in some sources
items.sourceNote: Some summaries cite April 1976; the exhibit avoids a fake exact day.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four objects explaining the month: public cabinets, home consoles, cartridges, handheld electronics and computing culture.

items.title: Fairchild Channel F cartridge architecture
items.body: The Channel F makes interchangeable home-console software a real retail category, even before Atari popularises the idea.

items.title: Dedicated Pong-style consoles
items.body: Telstar and similar machines show the first home wave: cheap, fixed-function and easy to explain.

items.title: Arcade cabinets as theatre
items.body: Sea Wolf's periscope and Night Driver's road illusion show that the cabinet is still part of the game design.
items.sourceNote: Sea Wolf and Night Driver context.

items.title: Handheld LED electronics
items.body: Mattel Auto Race suggests a private, pocketable branch of play before cartridge handhelds.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: There is still no normal videogame magazine shelf. Electronics and computing magazines are the map.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Networked play exists at the edges, but ordinary players live in a world of paper, cabinets and local knowledge.

items.title: Online play was not a home visitor experience
items.body: For most players in April 1976, game discovery happened through arcades, shops, magazines, clubs and word of mouth. Networked play existed at institutional edges, not as a normal domestic habit.
items.sourceNote: Included to avoid projecting later internet culture backward.

items.title: PLATO remains the hidden network
items.body: PLATO's games, messaging and shared terminals belong to the background of the era, but not to everyday UK consumer play.
items.sourceNote: PLATO context from early computer-game histories.

items.title: Paper is still the search engine
items.body: Magazines, adverts, catalogues and club newsletters are how players and hobbyists learn what exists.
items.sourceNote: BYTE, Creative Computing and electronics-magazine context.

items.title: The arcade is the social feed
items.body: Scores, queues and overheard advice make the cabinet itself a public information system.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A young medium, a patchy record, and a future still split between public machines and experimental home hardware.

items.title: The future was still public
items.body: Public play is still the centre: the cabinet has sound, controls, art, a coin slot and the authority of being out in the world.

items.title: The home shelf was changing shape
items.body: Home play is splitting: fixed TV games feel simple and immediate, while Channel F hints that a console might become a library.

items.title: Britain saw the edges first
items.body: From Britain, this still feels like amusements and electronics first, with the later home-computer culture not yet visible.

items.title: The record is patchy because the medium is young
items.body: There are few clean launch days, few consumer reviews and no settled games press. The museum label often has to say: year-level, regional, uncertain.
